| Random Forest (100 trees) | 22.1 | 0.76 |
| Small NN (2×32, dropout=0.3) | 23.5 | 0.73 |
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Step 3 — Tune Random Forest (Nested CV):**

- Best: `n_estimators=300, max_depth=8, min_samples_leaf=5`
- Nested CV MAE = 21.4 MPa, $R^2 = 0.78$
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Step 4 — Sensitivity analysis:** Laser power and scan speed account for 72% of variance (Sobol).
:::

::: {.notes}
This example walks through the complete workflow for a realistic materials science problem. We start with 120 specimens from 8 build plates and use GroupKFold with build plate as the group variable to prevent leakage. The baseline mean prediction gives an MAE of 45 MPa. Linear regression improves this to 28 MPa. We then compare four model families, all evaluated with the same grouped CV strategy. Random Forest performs best with a CV MAE of 22.1 MPa. We tune its hyperparameters using nested CV and achieve 21.4 MPa. Finally, Sobol sensitivity analysis reveals that laser power and scan speed together account for 72% of the variance in tensile strength. This information is directly useful for process optimization — these are the parameters to control most carefully.
:::

## 46. Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

### Lessons Learned

::: {.fragment}
| Pitfall | Consequence | Prevention |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Using test data for model selection | Optimistic performance estimate | Nested CV or separate holdout |
| Ignoring group structure | Data leakage, inflated $R^2$ | GroupKFold |
| No baseline model | Cannot judge if complex model is worthwhile | Always start with linear/mean |
| Over-engineering with deep learning | Overfitting, uninterpretable | Start simple, increase complexity if needed |
| Reporting training accuracy | Meaningless metric | Report CV or test accuracy only |
| Not checking learning curves | Wrong diagnosis | Always plot learning curves |
:::

::: {.fragment}
**The golden rule of applied ML:**

> "If your result seems too good to be true, it probably is. Check for data leakage."
:::

::: {.notes}
This slide summarizes the most common mistakes in applied ML for materials science. Each of these pitfalls is something I have seen repeatedly in published papers and student projects. The most dangerous is data leakage from ignoring group structure, because it produces results that look excellent but do not reflect real-world performance. Another common mistake is jumping to deep learning without establishing a baseline — you need to know what a simple model achieves before you can judge whether a complex model is justified. The golden rule is worth memorizing: if your R-squared is 0.99 on a materials dataset with 50 samples, you almost certainly have a bug, data leakage, or overfitting. Always be skeptical of results that seem too good.
:::

## 47. Exercise Preview

### What You Will Practice

::: {.fragment}
**Exercise 8a: Cross-Validation Strategies**

- Implement standard, stratified, and grouped K-Fold CV on an AM dataset
- Compare the CV scores to demonstrate the effect of ignoring group structure
- Plot learning curves to diagnose bias vs variance
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Exercise 8b: Regularization Comparison**

- Fit Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net to a materials dataset with 50 features
- Visualize the regularization path (coefficients vs $\lambda$)
- Identify which features are selected by Lasso
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Exercise 8c: Process Window Mapping**

- Train a Random Forest on LPBF process data
- Map the predicted density in Power-Speed space
- Perform sensitivity analysis using SHAP values
- Identify the robust operating point
:::

::: {.notes}
The exercises for this unit are designed to reinforce the key concepts through hands-on coding. Exercise 8a focuses on cross-validation — students will see firsthand how ignoring group structure leads to overly optimistic results. Exercise 8b compares regularization methods and visualizes how different regularizers affect the model coefficients. Exercise 8c ties everything together with a process window mapping exercise using real additive manufacturing data. Students will train a model, map the process window, perform sensitivity analysis, and identify a robust operating point. These exercises use scikit-learn and SHAP, which are standard tools in the field.
:::

## 48. Summary & Key Takeaways

::: {.fragment}
### 1. Generalization is Everything
Models must perform on **unseen data**. The bias-variance tradeoff governs this.
:::

::: {.fragment}
### 2. Validate Rigorously
Use **cross-validation** with appropriate grouping and stratification. Never evaluate on training data.
:::

::: {.fragment}
### 3. Regularize Intelligently
Combine explicit penalties (L1/L2), implicit methods (early stopping, augmentation), and architectural approaches (dropout) — tuned via CV.
:::

::: {.fragment}
### 4. Think About Robustness
Optimize for **stability**, not just peak performance. Use sensitivity analysis to understand which parameters matter.
:::

::: {.fragment}
### 5. Start Simple, Increase Complexity Only When Justified
Linear models → Random Forests → Neural Networks. Always compare to a baseline.
:::

::: {.notes}
These five takeaways capture the essence of this unit. Generalization is the only metric that matters for deployment. Cross-validation with appropriate grouping gives honest performance estimates. Regularization prevents overfitting, and the choice of regularization technique depends on the model type and the data. Robustness considerations transform ML from a prediction tool into a decision-support tool for manufacturing. And the principle of parsimony — start simple, add complexity only when the data justifies it — prevents most of the common pitfalls in applied ML.
:::

## 49. Looking Ahead

### Connections to Future Units

::: {.fragment}
| Concept from Unit 8 | Extended in | Topic |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Bias-Variance Tradeoff | Unit 9 | Ensemble Methods & Boosting |
| Cross-Validation | Unit 10 | Bayesian Model Selection |
| Regularization | Units 12-13 | Physics-Informed Neural Networks |
| Process Windows | Unit 11 | Uncertainty Quantification |
| SHAP & Sensitivity | Unit 14 | Explainable AI (XAI) |
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Next Unit (9):** We will see how **ensemble methods** (Boosting, Gradient-Boosted Trees) systematically reduce bias while controlling variance — building on the bias-variance framework from today.
:::

::: {.notes}
Unit 8 provides the foundation for several future units. The bias-variance framework helps us understand why ensemble methods work in Unit 9. Cross-validation connects to Bayesian model selection in Unit 10. Regularization is the precursor to physics-informed learning in Units 12 and 13, where domain knowledge provides the strongest form of regularization. Process windows connect to uncertainty quantification in Unit 11. And SHAP values are covered in depth in Unit 14 on explainable AI. In the next unit, we will explore ensemble methods, particularly gradient boosting, which systematically reduces bias while controlling variance.
:::

## 50. References

### Textbooks

- **@mcclarren2021machine**: Ch. 2 (Linear Models, Bias-Variance), Ch. 3 (Model Selection)
- **@neuer2024machine**: Ch. 4 (Supervised Learning Evaluation)
- **@sandfeld_materials_data_science**: Ch. 12 (Generalization), Ch. 16 (Cross-Validation)
- **@bishop2006pattern**: Ch. 3 (Linear Models for Regression, Regularization)
- **@goodfellow2016deep**: Ch. 7 (Regularization for Deep Learning)

### Further Reading

- **@murphy2012machine**: Ch. 7 (Linear Regression), Ch. 13 (Sparse Linear Models)
- Bergstra & Bengio (2012): "Random Search for Hyper-Parameter Optimization"
- Lundberg & Lee (2017): "A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions" (SHAP)
- Sobol (1993): "Sensitivity Estimates for Nonlinear Mathematical Models"

::: {#refs}
:::

::: {.notes}
These are the key references for this unit. McClarren Chapter 2 covers the bias-variance tradeoff with the polynomial regression example we discussed. Bishop Chapter 3 gives the mathematical treatment of regularization with excellent geometric intuitions. Goodfellow Chapter 7 covers regularization for deep learning including dropout and batch normalization. For the sensitivity analysis and SHAP material, the original papers by Sobol and Lundberg are the definitive references. Students should focus on McClarren Chapter 2 and the relevant sections of Sandfeld for the exam.
:::
